target.title: fix(agents): verify completion delivery target
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens ACP/subagent completion announcement delivery accounting so a completion is only counted as visibly delivered when the delivery evidence matches the requester route.

It:
- propagates outbound payload target metadata into delivery status
- validates automatic completion delivery against provider/account/channel/thread targets
- requires exact account/thread evidence for threaded origins
- preserves target metadata on failed payload outcomes for diagnostics
- keeps the current-chat generated-media compatibility path while avoiding explicit wrong-target matches

## Behavior addressed

Completion announcements can be injected through an internal/inter-session path while the original requester is a channel/thread. Broad checks such as “visible payload exists” or “message tool sent something” can count delivery as successful even when the visible delivery evidence belongs to another route.

This change makes completion delivery proof target-aware: wrong thread, missing thread, missing account, and provider mismatches no longer satisfy delivery for a concrete requester route.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Completion delivery is only counted as visibly delivered when runtime delivery evidence matches the requester route, including concrete account/thread target. Wrong-thread message-tool evidence must be rejected; exact-thread evidence must be accepted.
- **Real setup tested**: Disposable checkout of PR branch `anyech/openclaw:fix/acp-completion-thread-delivery` under `tmp/github-open-work/real-proof-20260603T073207Z/pr-89287`. A one-off non-production runtime harness (`proof-89287.ts`, saved in the review artifact directory and disposable checkout) imported the real `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` module and called `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` through the real announce dispatch/direct-delivery path. Synthetic session store/config/origin data used a fake Discord group/thread route; only the boundary gateway/transport deps were substituted to record and return delivery envelopes. No production Gateway/config/runtime/state or private deployment proof was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Terminal commands run in the disposable checkout were `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts` and `node --import tsx proof-89287.ts`; full command transcript is in `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/exact-commands-used.txt`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output / runtime log excerpt from `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/evidence/proof-89287.log`:

  ```json
  {
    "agentParams": {"deliver":false,"channel":"discord","to":"channel:proof-parent","threadId":"thread-proof-expected","sourceReplyDeliveryMode":"message_tool_only"},
    "wrong": {"delivered":false,"path":"direct","reason":"message_tool_delivery_missing","error":"completion agent did not use the message tool for message-tool-only delivery"},
    "correct": {"delivered":true,"path":"direct","phases":[{"phase":"direct-primary","delivered":true,"path":"direct"}]}
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: With the same synthetic requester route, message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-wrong` was rejected/flagged as missing target delivery, while message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-expected` was accepted as direct completion delivery. This exercised the repository runtime announce/direct-delivery implementation, not only an isolated matcher function.
- **What was not tested**: No live production Gateway, live Discord workspace, real bot token, or private deployment state was used. The outbound provider and gateway agent call were substituted only at the non-production boundary so the proof remains sanitized.

target.title: Guard cron announce delivery against implicit last routing
target.body:
## Summary

- reject ambiguous cron announce delivery that would resolve to implicit `last` routing when multiple message channels are configured
- preserve deterministic routes, including provider-prefixed `delivery.to`, explicit `delivery.channel`, `delivery.mode: "none"`, webhook failure destinations, and single-channel compatibility
- validate add/update paths that can create or activate ambiguous delivery outcomes, including failure destinations

## Why

In multi-channel deployments, cron follow-up/final-update jobs can be scheduled with implicit announce delivery and later fail during delivery resolution because no deterministic channel exists. This adds an admission-time guard so those jobs fail fast with an actionable validation error instead of silently scheduling an undeliverable result.

## Tests

- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.validation.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.validation.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.validation.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts`
  - 2 files, 43 tests passed
- `git diff --check main..HEAD`

## Notes

Full typecheck/build was left to CI; local broad validation was not run because the guarded heavy-run fallback image was unavailable locally.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** In multi-channel deployments, isolated/current/session cron `agentTurn` jobs with `announce` delivery could be accepted with implicit `last` routing, then later fail because no deterministic delivery channel existed. This patch adds admission-time validation so ambiguous announce routes fail fast while deterministic routes remain accepted.
- **Real setup tested:** Non-production staged OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, using a synthetic runtime config with two configured message channels (`slack`, `telegram`) and a temporary synthetic cron jobs file. The proof invoked the real gateway `cron.add` / `cron.update` handler paths and real `GatewayCronService` persistence path from this PR head. No production Gateway, production config, or production runtime state was touched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
PR80438_HEAD=7e2dbb524f16c9a9f02363891c2684cf73b64947 node --import tsx <non-production configured cron proof runner>
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.validation.test.ts -t '<six focused multi-channel add/update cases>' --reporter verbose
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "proof": "PR #80438 non-production configured multi-channel cron add/update proof",
  "head": "7e2dbb524f16c9a9f02363891c2684cf73b64947",
  "productionGatewayConfigRuntimeTouched": false,
  "config": { "channels": ["slack", "telegram"], "cronStore": "temporary synthetic jobs file" },
  "state": { "home": "temporary synthetic home", "runtime": "temporary synthetic state" },
  "cases": [
    { "case": "ambiguous implicit announce add is rejected", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.add", "ok": false },
    { "case": "provider-prefixed announce add is accepted", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.add", "ok": true },
    { "case": "ambiguous announce update is rejected", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.update", "ok": false },
    { "case": "provider-prefixed activation update is accepted", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.update", "ok": true },
    { "case": "disabling legacy ambiguous job is accepted", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.update", "ok": true },
    { "case": "re-enabling legacy ambiguous job is rejected", "result": "PASS", "method": "cron.update", "ok": false }
  ]
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** In a configured two-channel synthetic Gateway cron setup, ambiguous implicit `last` announce routes are rejected for both add and update, deterministic provider-prefixed announce routes are accepted for add and activation/update, disabling a legacy ambiguous job is accepted, and re-enabling/activating a legacy ambiguous job is rejected.
- **What was not tested:** This was intentionally non-production and does not claim a live production Gateway run. Broad typecheck/build remains covered by CI; this proof is scoped to the configured multi-channel cron add/update behavior gate.

target.title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
target.body:
## Summary

When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability.

**Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing.

**Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected.

A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback).

Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup.

Rebased to 2026.3.13.

---

## Real behavior proof — Telegram, live session (2026-05-22)

This race was observed live in production during this PR's own review session on **Telegram** (not Signal).

**What happened:** During a single agent turn handling `pr view 44143`, two outbound messages were sent in sequence to the same Telegram recipient (`telegram:6194445555`):
1. A long ClawSweeper analysis dump (~4KB)
2. A summary message referencing and synthesizing that analysis

**Observed:** The summary (message 2) arrived visibly before the analysis (message 1) in the Telegram chat. The recipient had to read the synthesis before the content it was synthesizing — the exact inversion this PR fixes.

**Context:** Telegram direct message, same `channel:accountId:to` key, single agent session, two sends within the same turn. No sub-agent involved — the race can happen within a single session turn when one send is large enough to lose the HTTP timing race against a smaller follow-up send.

**Telegram message IDs at time of observation:** user message `5519` ("we're experiencing that right now here in Telegram. Look at your last 3 messages"), preceded by the two inverted agent messages. Timestamp: 2026-05-22 ~15:00 EDT.

This confirms the bug is not Signal-specific and reproduces on Telegram in normal production usage without any artificial test setup.

target.title: fix(cli): retry logs.tail after journal fallback in logs follow
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `openclaw logs --follow` getting stuck on Linux systemd journal fallback after a transient local Gateway RPC outage or Gateway restart.

When implicit local `logs.tail` fails in follow mode, the CLI can use the active systemd journal as a bridge. However, after a journal payload was emitted, the follow loop permanently latched journal mode and stopped probing `logs.tail`. That meant log presentation could remain journal/console-style until the CLI was restarted, even after Gateway RPC recovered.

This keeps journal fallback temporary:
- preserve the journal cursor so repeated fallback reads do not duplicate journal output;
- do not permanently force journal mode after a journal fallback payload;
- retry `logs.tail` on the next follow iteration so recovered RPC switches back to normal Gateway log formatting/source.

## Related

- Related issue: #66841
- Follow-up to #85380, which added active systemd journal fallback to avoid stale configured-file following.

## Behavior

- Implicit local `openclaw logs --follow`:
  - transient RPC failure can emit active systemd journal fallback logs;
  - later successful `logs.tail` resumes normal RPC/file log output;
  - stale configured-file fallback is still not used for follow mode outages.
- Explicit `--url` behavior is unchanged.


## Reviewer / AI review map

- **Scope:** `openclaw logs --follow` source-selection state only; this does not change explicit `--url` behavior or the active systemd journal bridge itself.
- **Before:** one journal fallback payload latched follow mode into journal output and stopped retrying local Gateway `logs.tail`.
- **After:** journal fallback can bridge a transient outage, but the next follow iteration retries `logs.tail`; when RPC recovers, output returns to normal Gateway log formatting/source.
- **Non-overlap:** this is a CLI follow-loop state fix, not a Gateway config/runtime lifecycle change and not a general logging backend rewrite.
- **Review focus:** cursor preservation across repeated fallback reads, removal of the persistent journal latch, and resuming the Gateway cursor after recovered `logs.tail`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** After this patch, implicit local `openclaw logs --follow` retries `logs.tail` after a temporary systemd-journal fallback. Repeated fallback reads preserve the journal cursor, and a later successful `logs.tail` returns the follow session to normal Gateway RPC/file log output instead of staying journal-latched.
- **Real setup tested:** Disposable non-production OpenClaw checkout at PR head `aea512eec0ff0f6ecd096d05c0941041c05956c7`, using a synthetic profile/home, a loopback fake Gateway WebSocket that implements the real Gateway protocol handshake and `logs.tail` method, and fake `systemctl`/`journalctl` binaries at the OS boundary. No production Gateway, live runtime state, live config, or real system service was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the CLI startup bundle, started the fake Gateway on loopback, then ran the actual CLI follow command against the disposable profile with synthetic systemd tools first in `PATH`:

  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19081   HOME=<synthetic-home>   OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<synthetic-home>/.openclaw   OPENCLAW_PROFILE=proof   OPENCLAW_SYSTEMD_UNIT=openclaw-proof-gateway   PATH=<synthetic-bin>:<proof-bin>:$PATH   timeout 8s node openclaw.mjs logs --follow --plain --interval 50 --timeout 700
  ```

  The timeout wrapper only bounded the long-running follow command; the evidence below was emitted before timeout.
- **Evidence after fix:** The fake Gateway log recorded two initial failed `logs.tail` calls, then recovered Gateway RPC calls including cursor resume:

  ```text
  logs.tail call #1 -> terminate-for-fallback
  logs.tail call #2 -> terminate-for-fallback
  logs.tail call #3 -> recovered line cursor=10
  logs.tail call #4 -> cursor=10, recovered line cursor=20
  ```

  The CLI output showed journal fallback lines followed by recovered Gateway RPC/file log lines:

  ```text
  Log source: journalctl --user --boot --user-unit=openclaw-proof-gateway.service _PID=4242
  Service PID: 4242
  Service Unit: openclaw-proof-gateway.service
  first journal bridge line
  second journal bridge line
  2026-06-03T07:45:00.000+00:00 info gateway rpc recovered line cursor=10
  2026-06-03T07:45:00.000+00:00 info gateway rpc recovered line cursor=20
  ```

  The fake `journalctl` invocation log also showed cursor-preserving follow reads:

  ```text
  journalctl ... --show-cursor -n 200
  journalctl ... --show-cursor --after-cursor=s=abc
  journalctl ... --show-cursor --after-cursor=s=def
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The actual CLI process first fell back to the synthetic systemd journal when local `logs.tail` failed, kept retrying Gateway RPC instead of latching onto journal mode, then resumed Gateway log output after the fake Gateway recovered. The recovered RPC call used the Gateway cursor from the prior successful response (`cursor=10`), while journal fallback calls used `--after-cursor`, confirming both cursor paths advance independently.
- **What was not tested:** No production Gateway, no live OpenClaw runtime state, no live config, and no real systemd journal/service. The proof uses a disposable checkout plus synthetic loopback Gateway/systemd boundary so the CLI/runtime path is real while the external OS/Gateway boundary is controlled and public-safe.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

===== resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride) =====
  (no sessionKey)  default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  main session      default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  subagent session  default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

===== filterBootstrapFilesForSession — DEFAULTS (no tierOverride) =====
```

- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.

- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.

- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.

- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend execution must use the selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile rather than ambient Gemini CLI machine state. The backend must stage selected OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, select Gemini CLI OAuth auth mode through `settings.json`, clear inherited GCA override variables, and clean up the temporary home after use.

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, Node `v26.0.0`, real `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`, model `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. The proof used real local Gemini CLI OAuth credential material as input, but did not print raw access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or private account data.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw
PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' \
SOURCE_OAUTH_CREDS="$HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json" \
MODEL='gemini-3.1-flash-lite' \
PROMPT='Reply with exactly PR88748_AUTH_OK' \
pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-local-oauth-proof.mts 2>&1 \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log

sed -E \
  -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
  -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
  -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \
  -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' \
  /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log \
  > /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log

grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log || true
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== PR 88748 local Gemini CLI OAuth proof ===
profile=google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com
model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite
inherited_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
inherited_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=true
source_provider=google-gemini-cli
source_has_access=true
source_has_refresh=true
source_has_id_token=true
source_expired=false
prepared_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-iEQBYF
prepared_clearEnv=["GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA","GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN"]
clears_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
clears_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN=true
oauth_creds_shape={"type":"authorized_user","has_access_token":true,"has_refresh_token":true,"has_expiry_date":true,"token_type":"Bearer","has_id_token":true}
settings={"security":{"auth":{"selectedType":"oauth-personal"}}}
child_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA_present=false
child_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=false
real_gemini_exit=0
real_gemini_signal=
real_gemini_stdout_begin
{
  "session_id": "071a4f5c-f530-4242-9a9a-e1618c8abfc9",
  "response": "PR88748_AUTH_OK",
  "stats": {
    "models": {
      "gemini-3.1-flash-lite": {
        "api": {
          "totalRequests": 1,
          "totalErrors": 0,
          "totalLatencyMs": 1459
        },
        "tokens": {
          "input": 13057,
          "prompt": 13057,
          "candidates": 10,
          "total": 13067,
          "cached": 0,
          "thoughts": 0,
          "tool": 0
        }
      }
    },
    "tools": {
      "totalCalls": 0,
      "totalSuccess": 0,
      "totalFail": 0
    }
  }
}
real_gemini_stdout_end
real_gemini_stderr_begin
Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.

proof_result=PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The parent process had conflicting inherited GCA environment variables set. The patched backend returned `clearEnv` for those inherited variables, wrote the selected OAuth credential into an isolated Gemini CLI home, selected `oauth-personal` in Gemini CLI settings, and the real Gemini CLI subprocess ran with inherited GCA variables absent. The prompt returned the expected `PR88748_AUTH_OK` marker with `real_gemini_exit=0`, and cleanup removed the isolated home.

- **What was not tested**: The real behavior proof above covers the live OAuth/GCA override path. The API-key path and Vercel rejection path are covered by source-level tests in this PR; a live API-key prompt proof can be added separately if required. The proof intentionally avoids printing raw OAuth tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or account-private credential material.

- **Sanitization check**: `grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log` produced no matches.

target.title: ci: harden GitHub Actions supply-chain boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

This PR is release/publish supply-chain security-boundary hardening for high-risk GitHub Actions release, publish, live/e2e, and build-artifact paths.

Changes are intentionally scoped to CI/release plumbing:

- tighten release/publish workflow permissions and trusted-ref checks
- remove the ClawHub long-lived token fallback in favor of OIDC trusted publishing
- force plugin npm package publish to use OIDC trusted publishing
- keep plugin npm stable dist-tag mirror auth as a pre-publish availability guard without exposing npm auth to the package publish subprocess
- split stable dist-tag mirroring into a separate authenticated path
- harden cache/artifact boundaries so untrusted PR paths do not consume executable build caches before secret-bearing/testbox paths
- pin external Actions in the high-risk workflow scope to commit SHAs, with version comments retained for readability
- add focused regression coverage for the plugin npm token/precheck boundary

## Scope boundary

This is a release/publish supply-chain security-boundary hardening PR, not a test/CI-only PR and not a red-CI chasing PR. The workflow/script changes are limited to credential scoping, trusted-ref admission, OIDC/trusted publishing, cache/artifact trust boundaries, and external Action pinning in the touched high-risk release/publish/live/testbox paths.

It intentionally does not include unrelated fixes for known red `main` CI, lint/test flakes, or dependency advisories outside this boundary. If a check is also failing on `main` or is not caused by this diff, it remains out of scope for this PR.

## Details

### Publish / OIDC / ref hardening

- Add explicit default `permissions: {}` in high-risk workflows and move write permissions to job scope only where required.
- Require manual plugin publish workflows to be dispatched from `main` or `release/YYYY.M.D`.
- Require manual publish target overrides to be full 40-character commit SHAs reachable from `main` or `release/*`.
- Gate the write-scoped release publish path unconditionally on a trusted workflow ref.
- Remove the ClawHub token fallback so plugin ClawHub publish uses OIDC trusted publishing.
- Force plugin npm package publish to use OIDC trusted publishing.
- Preserve the stable-release mirror-auth precheck before package publish via a boolean availability flag, while keeping the long-lived npm token out of the package publish environment.
- Run actual stable dist-tag mirroring through `--mirror-dist-tags` as a separate npm-authenticated step/path.

### Cache / artifact boundary hardening

- Change the CI dist build cache to save-only after build, preventing an existing cache restore from overwriting freshly built artifacts before upload.
- Remove broad restore keys from the extension package boundary executable cache.
- Disable dist cache restore/save on pull request testbox runs and force PR runs to rebuild dist/UI before the testbox path.

### Action pinning

- Pin external Actions in the touched high-risk workflows to 40-character commit SHAs.
- Keep version comments such as `# v6` next to pins for maintainability.

## Validation

Initial stage validation included:

- `git diff --check`
- `actionlint` v1.7.11 on the 8 modified workflows
- PyYAML parse of the scoped workflows
- `python3 scripts/check-composite-action-input-interpolation.py`
- `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- focused invariants for trusted refs, cache predicates, SHA pins, and publish-token boundaries
- verification that the pinned `actions/cache/save` and `actions/cache/restore` commits contain their sub-action `action.yml` files

Latest current-head CI follow-up validation (2026-05-16):

- Corrected branch-update path to use SSH for GitHub pushes: `git@github.com:anyech/openclaw.git`, not HTTPS/PAT. The branch was force-updated with explicit `--force-with-lease` from `527378799c7a5507a6a01a2eb5ba549e7908e889` to `370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6`.
- Rebased the PR branch onto current upstream `main` at `532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93`. The earlier temporary Telegram type-fix fallback commit dropped during rebase because the patch contents are already upstream.
- Preserved this PR's release/publish hardening changes and added one follow-up pinning commit for the newly introduced release runtime parity `actions/checkout@v6` and `actions/upload-artifact@v4` steps.
- `bash -n scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh`
- PyYAML parse of the 8 changed workflow files
- `actionlint` v1.7.11 on the 8 changed workflow files
- `python3 scripts/check-composite-action-input-interpolation.py`
- focused audit that every external Action reference in the 8 changed high-risk workflows is pinned to a 40-character SHA (`113` checked references, `0` violations)
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- fake-npm proof for the plugin npm trusted-publishing token/precheck boundary (details below)

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: GitHub Actions release/publish/cache hardening plus the plugin npm token/precheck boundary. The latest follow-up rebases this branch onto current upstream `main`, uses SSH for the public branch push path, and keeps all touched high-risk workflow `uses:` references pinned to 40-character commit SHAs.
- Real setup tested: Disposable OpenClaw source checkout using this PR branch at commit `370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6`; current upstream `main` was `532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93`. No production OpenClaw Gateway/runtime/config/state was used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebases were performed locally in the disposable checkout; validation covered shell syntax, workflow YAML parsing, `actionlint`, composite-action input interpolation, conflict-marker scanning, and a focused external Action SHA-pin audit. The fake-npm proof harness created a synthetic stable package, put a fake `npm` first on `PATH`, confirmed `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --publish-package <synthetic-package>` refuses before package publish when mirror auth availability is not confirmed, confirmed the same package publish proceeds with only `OPENCLAW_NPM_DIST_TAG_MIRROR_AUTH_AVAILABLE=1` and no npm auth token, then ran `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --mirror-dist-tags <synthetic-package>` with synthetic npm auth. Because the disposable checkout did not have repository dependencies installed and the guarded heavy install path was unavailable, the proof used local dependency-resolution stubs only for missing `json5`/`tsdown`; `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_RUNTIME_BUILD=0` ensured the `tsdown` stub was not exercised.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal proof showed:

```text
Current PR head: 370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6
Current upstream main: 532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93
SSH push target: git@github.com:anyech/openclaw.git
Force-with-lease: 527378799c7a5507a6a01a2eb5ba549e7908e889 -> 370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6

Local validation after this patch:
- bash -n scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh: pass
- PyYAML parse of touched workflows: parsed 8 files
- actionlint v1.7.11 on touched workflows: pass
- composite-action input interpolation guard: pass
- external Action SHA-pin audit: PASS, 113 external action/workflow uses pinned across 8 touched workflows
- git diff --check: pass
- conflict-marker scan: pass

Trusted-publisher package publish precheck:
- npm dist-tag mirroring requires confirmed npm auth availability before package publish.
- stable package publish refuses before mirror auth availability is confirmed

Trusted-publisher package publish path:
- publish subprocess saw no NPM_TOKEN, NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, or NPM_CONFIG_USERCONFIG

Separate dist-tag mirror path:
- dist-tag mirror used a separate npm userconfig auth path for dist-tag add
```

- CI/lint follow-up: The branch is now rebased onto current upstream `main` and pushed via SSH with explicit `--force-with-lease`. GitHub CI is expected to rerun on the new head.
- Observed result after fix: Local proof confirms the plugin publish token/precheck boundary still holds after rebase, and the touched high-risk workflow set has no unpinned external Action references under the focused audit.
- What was not tested: No live npm publish, npm dist-tag mutation, ClawHub publish, release publish, or production Gateway run was performed. Those paths can publish or mutate external release state, so this PR intentionally uses fake npm plus disposable source-checkout validation and leaves the first real release/publish run to maintainer-controlled CI/release process.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the token-boundary/precheck fix, stable plugin publishes could publish before missing mirror auth was discovered in the later mirror step. Before the broader hardening patch, the touched workflows allowed tag/branch-pinned external Actions in the high-risk scope, PR testbox dist cache restore/save was enabled, and the ClawHub plugin publish path retained a long-lived token fallback.

## Rollback

If this breaks release or publish workflow behavior, revert this PR and re-run the affected workflow from the previous known-good release branch or tag.

The highest-risk rollback surface is expected to be:

- ClawHub OIDC-only publishing
- the stricter trusted workflow-ref gates
- narrowed job/workflow permissions
- plugin npm mirror-auth availability precheck plus the split package-publish/mirror flow

## Follow-up

The remaining known security debt is that stable npm dist-tag mirroring still requires long-lived npm auth. This PR narrows that token to the explicit mirror step/path and adds a pre-publish availability guard, but a later hardening pass should move mirroring into a more restricted workflow/environment or redesign it to avoid long-lived npm auth entirely.

target.title: fix(tasks): deliver ACP completions to bound Discord threads
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #84022 by delivering successful parent-review ACP task terminal updates to the explicitly bound Discord thread that originated the request.

This is a narrow replacement shape after #86672 and #88251 were closed:

- does **not** reopen or reuse #86672;
- does **not** change broad `room_event` suppression behavior;
- does **not** add a Discord-owned inbound/run timeout;
- does **not** use `Promise.race` or leave the underlying ACP run alive in the background;
- only permits direct visible terminal delivery when the task requester origin is an explicit Discord bound thread target.

## Behavior change

Before:

- delegated ACP parent-review tasks owned by a Discord channel/group session were always routed back through the requester session;
- for the #84022 thread-bound ACP shape, the ACP/session transcript could contain the terminal answer while the Discord thread stayed silent;
- task delivery could remain `session_queued` even though the originating Discord thread was explicit and deliverable.

After:

- existing parent-session routing is preserved for ordinary group/channel ACP completions;
- a successful ACP parent-review terminal update is delivered directly only when all of these are true:
  - requester origin channel is `discord`;
  - requester origin target starts with `channel:`;
  - requester origin has a concrete `threadId`;
  - the channel is deliverable.

The direct visible text uses the parent-review wording (`Background task ready for review...`) so the thread receives a terminal update without incorrectly implying the child result was fully verified/finalized by the parent.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A delegated ACP parent-review completion with an explicit Discord bound-thread requester origin sends the terminal update directly to that `threadId`; non-bound/non-qualifying paths remain queued through the parent session.
- **Real setup tested**: Disposable checkout of PR branch `anyech/openclaw:fix/bound-thread-acp-terminal-delivery-20260602` under `tmp/github-open-work/real-proof-20260603T073207Z/pr-89279`. Two non-production checks were run: (1) the focused task-registry runtime slice for the real ACP terminal delivery path with synthetic delivery runtime, and (2) a one-off harness over the real ACP spawn bootstrap delivery-plan code with synthetic Discord binding metadata. No production Gateway/config/runtime/state or private deployment proof was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Terminal commands run in the disposable checkout were `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts`, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts -t 'bound Discord thread|without an explicit bound Discord thread|routes .* ACP completion through the parent session|delivers non-delegated ACP completion' --reporter=verbose --pool=forks --maxWorkers=1`, and `node --import tsx proof-89279-direct.ts`; full command transcript is in `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/exact-commands-used.txt`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output / runtime log excerpts from `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/evidence/proof-89279-task-registry.log` and `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/evidence/proof-89279-direct.log`:

  ```text
  ✓ delivers delegated ACP completion directly to an explicitly bound Discord thread
  ✓ keeps delegated ACP completion queued without an explicit bound Discord thread ('missing-thread')
  ✓ keeps delegated ACP completion queued without an explicit bound Discord thread ('non-channel-target')
  ✓ keeps delegated ACP completion queued without an explicit bound Discord thread ('non-discord-channel')
  ✓ routes 'room channel' ACP completion through the parent session
  ✓ routes 'group' ACP completion through the parent session
  ✓ routes 'group topic' ACP completion through the parent session
  ✓ routes 'legacy Discord channel' ACP completion through the parent session
  ✓ routes 'legacy WhatsApp group' ACP completion through the parent session
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       10 passed | 68 skipped (78)
  ```

  ```json
  {
    "boundSession": {"channel":"discord","to":"channel:thread-bound-child","accountId":"acct-proof","threadId":"thread-bound-child","deliver":true},
    "nonBoundRun": {"deliver":false},
    "streamedRun": {"deliver":false}
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The bound Discord ACP path delivers to the explicit synthetic `threadId` and marks/sends the direct update in the task registry; missing thread, non-channel target, non-Discord, and ordinary parent-session ACP routes stay `session_queued` / `deliver:false` and do not send direct visible thread output.
- **What was not tested**: No live production Gateway, live Discord workspace, real bot token, or private deployment state was used. The outbound provider/transport was substituted at the boundary to keep proof non-production and sanitized. The one-off ACP spawn harness temporarily exported an internal helper only inside the disposable checkout and restored the file afterward; no code was pushed.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend execution must use the selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile rather than ambient Gemini CLI machine state. The backend must stage selected OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, select Gemini CLI OAuth auth mode through `settings.json`, clear inherited GCA override variables, and clean up the temporary home after use.

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, Node `v26.0.0`, real `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`, model `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. The proof used real local Gemini CLI OAuth credential material as input, but did not print raw access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or private account data.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw
PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' \
SOURCE_OAUTH_CREDS="$HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json" \
MODEL='gemini-3.1-flash-lite' \
PROMPT='Reply with exactly PR88748_AUTH_OK' \
pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-local-oauth-proof.mts 2>&1 \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log

sed -E \
  -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
  -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
  -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \
  -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' \
  /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log \
  > /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log

grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log || true
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== PR 88748 local Gemini CLI OAuth proof ===
profile=google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com
model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite
inherited_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
inherited_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=true
source_provider=google-gemini-cli
source_has_access=true
source_has_refresh=true
source_has_id_token=true
source_expired=false
prepared_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-iEQBYF
prepared_clearEnv=["GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA","GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN"]
clears_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
clears_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN=true
oauth_creds_shape={"type":"authorized_user","has_access_token":true,"has_refresh_token":true,"has_expiry_date":true,"token_type":"Bearer","has_id_token":true}
settings={"security":{"auth":{"selectedType":"oauth-personal"}}}
child_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA_present=false
child_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=false
real_gemini_exit=0
real_gemini_signal=
real_gemini_stdout_begin
{
  "session_id": "071a4f5c-f530-4242-9a9a-e1618c8abfc9",
  "response": "PR88748_AUTH_OK",
  "stats": {
    "models": {
      "gemini-3.1-flash-lite": {
        "api": {
          "totalRequests": 1,
          "totalErrors": 0,
          "totalLatencyMs": 1459
        },
        "tokens": {
          "input": 13057,
          "prompt": 13057,
          "candidates": 10,
          "total": 13067,
          "cached": 0,
          "thoughts": 0,
          "tool": 0
        }
      }
    },
    "tools": {
      "totalCalls": 0,
      "totalSuccess": 0,
      "totalFail": 0
    }
  }
}
real_gemini_stdout_end
real_gemini_stderr_begin
Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.
real_gemini_stderr_end
cleanup_removed_home=true
proof_result=PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The parent process had conflicting inherited GCA environment variables set. The patched backend returned `clearEnv` for those inherited variables, wrote the selected OAuth credential into an isolated Gemini CLI home, selected `oauth-personal` in Gemini CLI settings, and the real Gemini CLI subprocess ran with inherited GCA variables absent. The prompt returned the expected `PR88748_AUTH_OK` marker with `real_gemini_exit=0`, and cleanup removed the isolated home.

- **What was not tested**: The real behavior proof above covers the live OAuth/GCA override path. The API-key path and Vercel rejection path are covered by source-level tests in this PR; a live API-key prompt proof can be added separately if required. The proof intentionally avoids printing raw OAuth tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or account-private credential material.

- **Sanitization check**: `grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log` produced no matches.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Screenshots on fork branch `proof/pin-from-here-evidence` (linked below) plus gateway-log excerpts — `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn and the telegram mirror runs the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`), exactly once (post-hoc echo skipped by the handled-mark). - **Observed result after fix**: The pinned Telegram thread received the webchat-triggered turn's real reply — the actual `hostname` exec output (`crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`) — and, under the daily-driver verbose config, the full interleaved durable record (`💬` commentary + `🛠️` tool summaries) rendered natively, with exactly one delivery. - **What was not tested**: A *live* current-head capture of **destination revocation** (the gate is unit-proven and the post-hoc fall-through is closed, but the test-rig group-disable mechanism proved finicky to film). Multi-account routing IS now proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: fix(session): clear stale auto fallback origins
target.body:
## Summary

- Clear direct session auto-fallback pins when their recorded fallback origin no longer matches the current configured primary.
- Apply the stale-origin check in both early reply model selection and the later model-selection state path so normal turns retry the primary instead of staying pinned to the fallback.
- Add a regression test for the polluted-origin shape from #92776.

Fixes #92776.

## Verification

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Auto-created fallback model pins with polluted `modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider` / `modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel` no longer keep normal turns on the fallback indefinitely when the current configured primary differs from the stored origin.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout at `b63a5e3df4f062be5206015c4d09673a4ae55eac` on macOS with Node `v24.15.0` and pnpm `11.2.2`, running the production model-selection module through `node --import tsx` against a temporary session-store entry shaped like #92776.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { createModelSelectionState } from "./src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts";

const sessionKey = "agent:main:telegram:direct:real-proof";
const sessionEntry = {
  sessionId: "session-real-proof",
  updatedAt: Date.now(),
  providerOverride: "anthropic",
  modelOverride: "claude-opus-4-7",
  modelOverrideSource: "auto",
  modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider: "anthropic",
  modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel: "claude-haiku-4-5",
};
const sessionStore = { [sessionKey]: sessionEntry };

const result = await createModelSelectionState({
  cfg: {},
  agentCfg: undefined,
  sessionEntry,
  sessionStore,
  sessionKey,
  defaultProvider: "anthropic",
  defaultModel: "claude-opus-4-8",
  primaryProvider: "anthropic",
  primaryModel: "claude-opus-4-8",
  provider: "anthropic",
  model: "claude-opus-4-7",
  hasModelDirective: false,
});

console.log(JSON.stringify({
  selected: { provider: result.provider, model: result.model },
  resetModelOverride: result.resetModelOverride,
  resetModelOverrideRef: result.resetModelOverrideRef,
  persistedEntry: {
    providerOverride: sessionStore[sessionKey].providerOverride ?? null,
    modelOverride: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverride ?? null,
    modelOverrideSource: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideSource ?? null,
    originProvider: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider ?? null,
    originModel: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel ?? null,
  },
}, null, 2));
EOF
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output from that command:

```json
{
  "selected": {
    "provider": "anthropic",
    "model": "claude-opus-4-8"
  },
  "resetModelOverride": true,
  "resetModelOverrideRef": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7",
  "persistedEntry": {
    "providerOverride": null,
    "modelOverride": null,
    "modelOverrideSource": null,
    "originProvider": null,
    "originModel": null
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The selected model is the current primary `anthropic/claude-opus-4-8`, `resetModelOverride` is true for the stale fallback pin, and the stored override plus fallback-origin fields are cleared from the session entry.

What was not tested: I did not run a live provider-outage reproduction or call a real Anthropic account; this proof exercises the production OpenClaw model-selection path on a local checkout with the persisted session-state shape from #92776.
